region_of_origin: Singapore (product of Nestlé; widely adopted as a cultural staple in Singapore, Malaysia, and Southeast Asia)
food_subgroup: Chocolate malt hot and cold beverages

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a Milo-specific entry; 'chocolate malt beverage' (FOODON_03301099) is the closest defensible class match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A003K covers cocoa/chocolate-based beverages; no Milo-specific code exists. A003K is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226365003 'Malted drink' is the closest defensible concept; no Milo brand-specific code exists.

preparation_method: dissolved in hot or cold water or milk

serving.description: 1 cup prepared with water (200 ml, ~3 heaped teaspoons powder ~22 g)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of Milo is approximately 22 g of powder (about 3 heaped teaspoons) dissolved in 200 ml of hot or cold water or low-fat milk, as recommended on the Nestlé Milo product label.

nutrient_source.title: Milo Chocolate Malt Beverage Powder — Nestlé Singapore product nutrition panel; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central: Milo Chocolate Malt Beverage Mix (FDC ID 2003571)
nutrient_source.organization: Nestlé Singapore / USDA FoodData Central (Branded Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values are per 100 g of Milo powder as reported on the Nestlé Singapore product label and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central branded entry for Milo Chocolate Malt Beverage Mix. Minor rounding differences between markets are noted; Singapore label values are used as primary.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for chocolate malt beverage powder prepared with water
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database lists Milo powder made up with water at GI ≈ 55 (low). This is corroborated by Foster-Powell et al. (2002) AJCN 76:5–56 which reports chocolate malt beverage drinks in the GI 50–60 range. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 14.7 g carb in 22 g powder) / 100 ≈ 8.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (210 mg per 100 g powder; ~46 mg per standard 22 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 22 g serving of Milo powder contributes approximately 46 mg sodium, which is modest, but individuals with hypertension who consume multiple servings daily or prepare Milo with full-cream milk should monitor cumulative sodium and saturated fat intake and prefer preparation with water or low-fat milk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (40 g sugar per 100 g powder) and low glycemic index (GI 55)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Milo powder contains 40 g of sugar per 100 g and has a low GI of approximately 55; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one standard serving (22 g powder in water), avoid adding extra sugar, and account for the approximately 14.7 g of available carbohydrate per serving within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (3.6 g per 100 g powder) and added sugar load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 22 g serving of Milo powder provides approximately 0.8 g of saturated fat and 8.8 g of sugar; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prepare Milo with water rather than full-cream milk to limit saturated fat intake, and restrict consumption to one serving per day to avoid excessive added sugar contributing to dyslipidaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Milo is a chocolate malt beverage powder manufactured by Nestlé, originally developed in Australia in 1934 and now deeply embedded as a cultural staple in Singapore, Malaysia, and across Southeast Asia. The powder is made from malt barley extract, cocoa, sugar, skim milk powder, vegetable oil, and a blend of vitamins and minerals including calcium, iron, and B-vitamins. It is typically prepared by dissolving 3 heaped teaspoons (approximately 22 g) in hot or cold water or low-fat milk. In Singapore, Milo is served at hawker centres, kopitiams, and cafes in several popular forms: Milo panas (hot Milo), Milo ais (iced Milo), Milo O (Milo with water and no milk), Milo dinosaur (iced Milo topped with a generous heap of undissolved Milo powder), and Milo godzilla (Milo dinosaur with added ice cream). The drink has a sweet, malty, chocolatey flavour with a smooth texture. Nutritionally, 100 g of Milo powder provides approximately 385 kcal, 73.5 g carbohydrate (of which 40 g is sugar), 6.6 g protein, 6.3 g fat, and is notably enriched with calcium (900 mg/100 g), iron (12 mg/100 g), and multiple B-vitamins. The glycemic index of Milo prepared with water is approximately 55 (low GI). Clinically, the high sugar content warrants portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease, and preparation method (water vs. full-cream milk) significantly affects the overall saturated fat and calorie load of the final beverage.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cup of Milo?
What is the GI of Milo?
How much sugar is in Milo?
Milo panas vs Milo ais nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced directly from the Nestlé Singapore Milo product nutrition panel and corroborated by the USDA FoodData Central branded food entry (FDC ID 2003571). GI is supported by the University of Sydney GI Database and Foster-Powell et al. (2002). The food is a well-documented commercial product with consistent labelling across markets.
notes: Values are per 100 g of Milo powder (dry, as sold). Prepared-beverage nutrition will differ significantly depending on the amount of powder used and whether water, low-fat milk, or full-cream milk is used. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per Nestlé Singapore product label. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown is not reported on the Singapore product label and is therefore set to -1. Folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are not reported on the Singapore product label and are set to -1. Calcium enrichment is a key product feature (900 mg per 100 g powder). The cuisine field is set to 'Singaporean' reflecting the food's deep cultural adoption in Singapore, though the product was originally developed by Nestlé Australia. Milo dinosaur and Milo godzilla are Singapore-specific hawker preparations that add significantly more powder or ice cream and will have higher calorie and sugar loads than the standard serving.
